name: European Union

governance.decision_making: Qualified majority voting (most areas); unanimity required for foreign policy and taxation

strategic_priorities: Single market deepening and digital single market completion
European Green Deal and Fit for 55 climate package
Strategic Autonomy initiative — defense and technology
Capital Markets Union and Banking Union consolidation
Enlargement: Ukraine, Moldova, Western Balkans accession process
AI Act implementation and digital regulation leadership
summary: The European Union is the world's largest single market and the foremost example of supranational economic and political integration. Despite sluggish growth in 2025, the bloc is pressing ahead with defense integration, green transition investment, and digital regulation that sets global standards. Geopolitical pressure from Russia's war in Ukraine and transatlantic trade tensions have accelerated moves toward strategic autonomy.
recent_developments: ReArm Europe plan approved: EUR 150 billion defense investment fund to be mobilized through 2030, partially financed by common EU bonds
Ukraine and Moldova formally opened accession negotiations in 2024; Ukraine reconstruction fund at EUR 50 billion disbursing through 2027
EU AI Act entered full enforcement in early 2026, making the EU the first major jurisdiction with comprehensive AI regulation
ECB cut deposit rate to 2.25% by Q1 2026 after inflation returned to target, supporting fragile recovery across the eurozone
Poland assumed EU Council Presidency in January 2026, prioritizing security, defense industry, and eastern enlargement
risks: Widening north-south fiscal divergence; Italian debt sustainability under watch
Hungarian and Slovak governments resisting EU consensus on Ukraine support
US tariff threats creating uncertainty for European export-oriented industries
Energy price volatility persisting as full Russia gas decoupling completes
